variables.content: Because direct thermodynamic-limit extrapolation of the discrete-angle DMC-determined Fermi liquid parameters proved unreliable, a set of “best available” numerical recommendations for the Fermi liquid parameters was assembled by combining independently published QMC results for related quantities: quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ obtained from DMC energy-band calculations and smooth QMC-based parameterizations of the ground-state correlation energy.  Using the literature effective masses together with compressibility and spin-susceptibility values derived from the total-energy parameterizations and the Landau relations yields recommended values such as, for the paramagnetic 2D HEG: at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}^{s}=-0.495(2)$, $F_{0}^{a}=-0.346(2)$, $F_{1}^{s}=-0.053(3)$; at $r_{s}=5$ and $10$ analogous recommended values are provided; and for the ferromagnetic 2D HEG recommended values include at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}=-0.428(2)$ and $\bar{F}_1=-0.159(3)$.  These assembled values combine ground-state energy parameterizations and published effective masses to provide pragmatic numerical recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable.

Landau Fermi-liquid theory relates low-temperature thermodynamic and response functions of a homogeneous interacting Fermi system to quasiparticle parameters: specifically, the quasiparticle effective mass m^* is linked to the linear-in-T specific-heat coefficient γ = C/T, the compressibility is related to the symmetric Landau parameter F_0^{s}, and the spin susceptibility χ is related to the antisymmetric Landau parameter F_0^{a} via χ = (m^*/m) χ_0/(1+F_0^{a}), where χ_0 is the ideal-gas susceptibility; the assumption used in extracting Landau parameters from the experimental data is that, at the low temperatures T ≲ 0.06 T_F and small polarizations probed in the normal-phase measurements, the unitary Fermi gas lies within the regime of validity of Landau Fermi-liquid phenomenology so that these relations can be applied quantitatively to determine m^*/m, F_0^{s} and F_1^{s} from measured compressibility, specific heat and magnetic susceptibility.

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

At unitarity (1/k_Fa = 0) the combination of the measured grand-canonical dimensionless magnetic susceptibility ṽχ, independent thermodynamic determinations of the compressibility and of the linear-in-T specific-heat coefficient, and Landau Fermi-liquid relations yields a Fermi-liquid parametrization of the low-lying excitations characterized by an effective mass m^*/m = 1.13 and by symmetric Landau parameters consistent with prior determinations (F_0^{s} ≃ −0.40 and F_1^{s} ≃ 0.39), and provides a determination of the antisymmetric (spin) Landau parameter
      $$
      F_0^{a}=\frac{m^*}{m}\,\widetilde{\chi}(0)^{-1}-1=1.1(1),
      $$
      where ṽχ(0) is the dimensionless susceptibility at unitarity in the canonical (or in the appropriately transformed grand-canonical) convention used throughout this work; the positive value F_0^{a}>0 indicates net magnetic correlations that favor singlet spin configurations.

At the unitary limit (infinite scattering length), combining a measured zero-temperature dimensionless spin susceptibility $\tilde{\chi}(0)$ for the normal phase with previously measured low-temperature compressibility and specific heat values yields a set of Landau Fermi-liquid parameters and quasiparticle properties: the quasiparticle effective mass is $m^*=1.13\,m$ (with m the bare fermion mass), the symmetric Landau parameters are $F_0^s\approx -0.40$ and $F_1^s\approx 0.39$ (the latter value having been reported by prior thermodynamic measurements), and the antisymmetric (spin) Landau parameter is given by the Landau relation
      $$
      F_0^a=\frac{m^*}{m}\,\tilde{\chi}(0)^{-1}-1,
      $$
      which evaluates to $F_0^a=1.1(1)$ when inserting $m^*=1.13\,m$ and the measured $\tilde{\chi}(0)$ with its uncertainty; the positive value $F_0^a>0$ corresponds to magnetic correlations that favor spin-singlet configurations.

Functional-derivative formulas for the Landau interaction function $f_{\sigma\sigma'}(\cos\theta)$ were evaluated using the same static many-body local fields and response functions to obtain the symmetric and antisymmetric Landau parameters $F_{0}^{s}$ and $F_{0}^{a}$. From these parameters and the computed effective mass $m^{*}$ one obtains the effective Landé factor and Fermi-liquid response ratios via the standard relations
      $$
      \frac{g^{*}}{g}=\frac{1}{1+F_{0}^{a}},\qquad \frac{\chi_{s}}{\chi_{s}^{0}}=\frac{m^{*}/m}{1+F_{0}^{a}},\qquad \frac{K}{K_{0}}=\frac{m^{*}/m}{1+F_{0}^{s}}.
      $$
      The main numerical findings are: (i) RPA values for the compressibility ratio $K/K_{0}$ agree well with available diffusion-QMC results across the metallic density range considered; (ii) the spin-susceptibility enhancement $\chi_{s}/\chi_{s}^{0}$ computed using the Dyson-equation effective mass $m_{D}^{*}$ is in excellent quantitative agreement with available QMC data, whereas use of the on-shell-approximation mass $m_{OSA}^{*}$ leads to larger deviations; and (iii) these Fermi-liquid response ratios are sensitive to the adopted effective mass, so that accurate determination of $m^{*}$ is crucial for reliable predictions of $\chi_{s}$ and $K$.

For a Fermi liquid of spin-$\tfrac{3}{2}$ fermions with quasiparticle density of states $N_{f}$ at the Fermi energy and well-defined Landau interaction parameters $F_{\ell}^{(\cdot)}$ decomposed into $SO(5)$ scalar/vector/tensor channels, the compressibility-like susceptibility $\chi_{\rho}$ and the $SO(5)$ spin-susceptibility-like coefficient $\chi_{sp}$ entering the composite-boson effective Hamiltonian are renormalized according to the Landau expressions
      $$
      \chi_{\rho}=\frac{N_{f}}{4(1+F_{0}^{s})},\qquad \chi_{sp}=\frac{N_{f}}{4(1+F_{0}^{t})},
      $$
      and the ratio of spin superfluid density to superfluid density is renormalized as
      $$
      \frac{\rho_{sp}}{\rho_{s}}=\frac{1+F_{1}^{v}/3}{1+F_{1}^{s}/3}.
      $$
      These relations assume the system is in the Fermi-liquid regime where standard Landau theory applies and the relevant Landau parameters are finite and well defined.
      [39]

The extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses exhibit distinct density dependences depending on spin polarization: for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10 the extrapolated effective mass remains close to unity (\\(m^{*}\\approx1\\)), indicating no significant interaction-induced mass enhancement at these densities for the clean continuum 2D HEG model considered; by contrast, for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the extrapolated effective mass decreases as the density is lowered, for example taking the values \\(0.85(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=1\\), \\(0.74(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=5\\), and \\(0.70(3)\\) at \\(r_{s}=10\\), a trend that is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements on spin-polarized two-dimensional systems reported in the literature (e.g., Padmanabhan et al.).

Define the quasiparticle density of states ρ_0≡∮_{FS} dS / v_F obtained by integrating the inverse Fermi velocity around the Fermi surface, and denote by χ_0 and κ the uniform spin susceptibility and compressibility respectively. The standard Landau Fermi-liquid relations χ_0 ∼ ρ_0 / (1+F_0^a) and κ ∼ ρ_0 / (1+F_0^s) with Landau parameters F_0^{a,s} are assumed to remain operationally applicable in the proximate metallic regime even as the quasiparticle residue Z→0 and the effective mass diverges logarithmically; under this assumption divergences of F_0^{a,s} can be inferred by inserting the computed infrared behaviors of ρ_0, χ_0, and κ into these relations, acknowledging that this is a phenomenological extrapolation of Fermi-liquid relations into a marginal or near-critical regime.
